comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around Kagi's Small Web product, with users appreciating the concept of curated, smaller-scale web content but criticizing its narrow definition and limitations. Some users compare it to StumbleUpon, note issues with AI-generated content, and express concerns about discoverability and relevance compared to Google.

comments.author: hamdingers
comments.text:
I'm a Kagi search/assistant user and advocate but the "small web" product is a frustrating misnomer.

To me the small web is any little website that was created to be interesting rather than to sell me something. That includes stuff like neocities, "shrine" type sites, single purpose sites, fandom portals, web experiments, etc.

Unfortunately Kagi's definition of "small web" is: blog or webcomic. You must have an RSS feed and it must have recent posts. That rules out so much interesting stuff I don't understand the point.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately identifies the product's restrictive scope and offers a clear critique based on a reasonable definition of the 'small web'.

comments.author: wei03288
comments.text: The discoverability problem for small sites is a distribution issue more than a quality issue. Most independent blogs produce genuinely useful content, but search engines have structurally deprioritized them in favor of sites with SEO budgets and link networks. What's interesting about Kagi's approach is that it doesn't try to fix the ranking algorithm — it just adds a separate signal. The question is whether that signal stays clean as it scales, or whether it becomes another thing people optimize for.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced understanding of search engine dynamics and the potential impact of Kagi's approach on small site visibility.

comments.author: freetonik
comments.text:
On a similar note, I maintain and grow a manually curated collection of personal blogs with valid RSS feeds: https://minifeed.net/blogs

The criteria is simple: human-written (as much as I can validate myself), in English (for now), with valid RSS feed, and not a micro-blog (so, more than just feed of links or short tweet-like messages).

Similar to Kagi's Small Web viewer, or StumbleUpon-style viewer: you can get a random listing of blogs [1] or a random listing of posts from all blogs [2]. Feeds and posts are indexed, so full-text search works across all blogs. When possible and permitted by robots.txt, text is scraped for searching, so even if some text is omitted in the RSS feed by the author, search should work.

Though I do plan to implement a similar "view one random post at source" kind of view, soon.

UPD: Feel free to submit a blog, including your own! [3]

[1] https://minifeed.net/blogs/by/random

[2] https://minifeed.net/global/random

[3] https://minifeed.net/suggest
comments.analysis: Offers a relevant alternative project that shares goals with Kagi's Small Web, demonstrating practical experience and constructive contribution.

comments.author: lich_king
comments.text:
I think it shows the limits of hand curation. It's a tiny, human-reviewed slice of the "small web", only allowing a subset of blogs... but if you select the "programming" category and click around for a short while, you get a fair amount of obvious AI slop.

I don't think it's Kagi's fault, but I guess it's depressing in a way. A lot of "small web" bloggers dream of being a part of the "big web", and when they get a cheat button, they have no second thoughts about mashing it.
comments.analysis: Insightfully critiques the challenges of curation and the infiltration of AI content, reflecting realistic concerns about content quality.

comments.author: ArtificeAccount
comments.text:
I've been using the Kagi search engine for months now and I'm not impressed. I bought into it because there were a lot of posts saying that it was "just like old Google" but this has not been my experience. It's the same as new Google, you can type in what you're looking for exactly and you'll get random sort-of related websites.

I remember when you could half-remember a comment from a website, type that into Google, and get taken to the article you were looking for. That was back in like 2010. To me that's the old, and useful, search engine that I want.
comments.analysis: Expresses user dissatisfaction with search relevance, echoing common critiques of modern search engines, though somewhat subjective.

comments.author: hitekker
comments.text:
Kagi lost focus. I pay for their search and I’m getting close to giving up. I’ve noticed several times that Google Search gets more relevant results for the same query. On top of having a better search AI.

Whimsical detours like this feels like they’re operating aa early Google minus the killer product.
comments.analysis: Reflects a critical user perspective on Kagi's search quality compared to Google, highlighting concerns about product focus.

comments.author: unbindableisaac
comments.text:
Bit bummed. The first random page I landed on was a really interesting article for me. The custom cursor (well why not) had me struggling to following a link, and instinctively I refreshed the page. I ended up somewhere else in the haystack with ostensibly no way back to that particular article.

Perhaps I'm yelling into the void here, but what would be great is when first landing at kagi.com/smallweb, the url query parameter would be somehow set, as it is when "Next Post" is clicked.
comments.analysis: Provides useful user experience feedback suggesting improvements to navigation and usability.

comments.author: barbazoo
comments.text:
Love Kagi and most things they do, don't get me wrong, but this is a bit ironic:

https://kagi.com/smallweb/?url=https://pliutau.com/reading-l...

> This page is auto-generated from Github Actions workflow that runs every day at night and fetches the 5 latest articles from each of my favorite blogs.
comments.analysis: Accurately describes the technical process behind content generation, adding transparency to the product's operation.

comments.author: modernerd
comments.text:
Great idea. Index is based on submitted sites:

https://github.com/kagisearch/smallweb/blob/main/smallweb.tx...

There is also Small Comic:

https://kagi.com/smallweb/?comic

https://github.com/kagisearch/smallweb/blob/main/smallcomic....

And Small YouTube:

https://kagi.com/smallweb/?yt

https://github.com/kagisearch/smallweb/blob/main/smallyt.txt
comments.analysis: Provides factual information about the product's structure and data sources, useful for understanding scope.

comments.author: starkparker
comments.text: First impressions: My first five pages were stallman.org, a paywalled cybersec newsletter, a German-language blog, an AI-generated blog post ad for a cattle fencing service, and a blog republishing a Disney Parks press release
comments.analysis: Offers anecdotal evidence of content variety and quality issues, highlighting the mixed nature of the curated index.
